CogniLeapAI is an AI-powered adaptive learning platform that transforms static academic PDFs into a closed-loop personalized study system. The platform combines multi-provider AI routing, evidence-based spaced repetition, and autonomous study planning into a single web application, making advanced AI-driven study tools accessible to students without subscription paywalls or manual content authoring. The system addresses the gap that students spend ten or more hours preparing study material per document, retain less than thirty percent of passively read content after a week, and lack tools that combine AI generation with rigorous learning science.

The platform is built on Next.js 15.5, React 19, TypeScript, and Supabase PostgreSQL with row-level security, with a four-provider AI router covering Google Gemini, OpenRouter, LaoZhang, and Kie.ai with automatic three-tier fallback. From any uploaded PDF, the platform concurrently generates six categories of study material: summaries, study guides, active-learning notes, flashcards, multiple-choice quizzes, and node-based mind maps. Retention is managed through a four-layer SM-2 spaced repetition framework (Absorb, Recognize, Retrieve, Mastered) extended with response-time-aware interval adjustment, per-topic AI multipliers, and post-session weakness scoring.

An Adaptive AI Study Agent produces exam-aware multi-day study plans, adapts them in real time based on per-topic performance and stuck-card detection, and embeds structured action markers that drive autonomous tool generation, card synchronization, and review session orchestration. User API keys are encrypted with AES-256-GCM. Benchmarks demonstrate a 99.5% AI request success rate, sub-second streaming chat response, and a 99.8% reduction in study material preparation time compared to manual workflows.
Keywords: Educational Technology, Multi-Model AI, Spaced Repetition, SM-2, Active Recall, Adaptive Learning, Generative AI, Personalized Learning

CHAPTER 1: INTRODUCTION
1.1 Detailed Problem Definition

Every academic semester places an enormous reading burden on students. Undergraduate and postgraduate learners are routinely expected to process hundreds of pages of textbooks, research papers, lecture notes, and supplementary academic material, while simultaneously preparing for examinations, completing coursework, and participating in laboratory or project work. The dominant study method across most institutions remains linear, manual reading of these materials accompanied by hand-written notes. This approach scales poorly. Retention drops sharply within hours of reading without active reinforcement, and the preparation time required to manually convert raw documents into usable study material is consistently underestimated by both students and educators.

Static PDF readers, by far the most common tool in the student's workflow, contribute almost nothing to learning beyond display. They do not validate comprehension, generate study material, monitor recall performance, or adapt to individual gaps in understanding. A student who reads a 200-page chapter still has to either trust passive exposure or manually build flashcards, summaries, and quizzes, an exercise that frequently consumes ten or more hours per document. The opportunity cost of that time is considerable, since time spent producing study material is time not spent on application, problem solving, or revision.

Commercial AI tools that could in principle address these limitations are largely positioned behind monthly subscription paywalls. ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, and similar offerings price themselves at fifteen to twenty United States dollars per month, which is out of reach for a substantial portion of the student population, especially in price-sensitive academic markets. Free tiers of these products typically impose strict daily limits and exclude access to the more capable model variants that are best suited to long-document study workflows.

More fundamentally, even when access is available, most general-purpose AI assistants are not designed as structured learning systems. They are question-answering interfaces. They will answer document-level queries reasonably well in a single session, but they cannot persist context across separate sessions in any meaningful way, cannot produce organized study materials in multiple formats from one prompt, do not monitor recall performance over time, do not schedule spaced reviews tied to forgetting-curve estimates, and do not generate personalized study plans that respond to performance data.

The problem space addressed by this project can therefore be summarized along the following six dimensions. First, students struggle to extract key information from lengthy PDFs efficiently using existing tools. Second, generic AI assistants are unable to reference specific passages in source documents in a way that supports verifiable study. Third, the time cost of manually creating study materials such as flashcards, quizzes, and structured study guides is prohibitively high. Fourth, no widely available platform integrates spaced repetition scheduling for long-term retention with AI-generated study material. Fifth, adaptive study planning that responds to individual performance data is largely absent from the consumer student tooling market. Sixth, cost-transparent AI access suited to students operating on limited budgets is rare; pricing is typically opaque or tied to consumer subscription models that do not match academic usage patterns.

This project, CogniLeapAI, was conceived as a direct response to these six dimensions of the problem. It is positioned as a unified web platform that converts uploaded PDF documents into a closed-loop adaptive learning system, with AI-driven content generation, an evidence-based active recall framework, and an autonomous study planning agent operating on the same underlying data.
1.2 Justification of Problem

The justification for building CogniLeapAI rests on three converging observations from the educational technology landscape, the research literature on learning, and the current state of artificial intelligence.

First, the educational technology market is bifurcated. On one side are passive document readers and note-taking applications, which add little value beyond what a basic PDF viewer or word processor already offers. On the other side are enterprise learning management systems, which are typically procured by institutions and designed for course administration rather than personal study workflows. Between these two extremes there is no widely adopted middle ground that combines AI-driven personalization with rigorous learning science for the individual student. The platforms that come closest are subscription-based productivity tools that treat study as a generic information task rather than a longitudinal learning process.

Second, the research literature on spaced repetition and active recall is unusually robust. Karpicke and Roediger demonstrated that spaced retrieval practice produces significantly greater long-term retention than repeated re-reading. Dunlosky and colleagues ranked spaced practice and retrieval practice as the two most effective learning techniques in a comprehensive review of ten widely used methods. Cepeda et al. reported that distributed practice produces retention gains exceeding two hundred percent compared to massed practice in their meta-analysis of the spacing effect. These findings have been replicated repeatedly across decades and educational contexts. Despite this, the most widely used commercial study tools, including Anki and Quizlet, require the student to manually author every flashcard. This places the entire content-creation burden on the learner and discourages exactly the kind of broad coverage that spaced repetition is most effective for.

Third, recent advances in large language models, particularly the introduction of one-million-token context windows in Google's Gemini family, have removed a long-standing technical barrier to document-level AI assistance. Previously, processing an entire academic document required chunking strategies and retrieval-augmented pipelines, both of which introduce errors at the retrieval step and degrade output coherence. The availability of full-document context, combined with multi-model routing across cost-optimized providers, makes it technically and economically feasible to build a personalized study platform that processes entire textbooks in one pass without chunking, summarization shortcuts, or vector database overhead.

Taken together, these three observations identify a clear gap that this project is positioned to fill. The educational technology market lacks an integrated personal study platform; the learning science literature provides a well-validated set of techniques that have not been operationalized for general consumer use; and the underlying AI capability has only recently become available at a price point and quality level suitable for student workloads.
1.3 Need for the New System

The need for a new system arises directly from the unmet requirements identified in the problem analysis. Each of the following capabilities is unmet, partially met, or met only at prohibitive cost by existing solutions, and the combination of all of them in a single integrated platform is not available in any product known to the project team at the time of design:

An AI-driven study material generation system that produces six distinct types of study material (summaries, study guides, notes, flashcards, quizzes, and mind maps) from any uploaded document, with concurrent generation and independent progress tracking for each type.

A four-layer spaced repetition framework built on the SM-2 algorithm, with response-time-aware interval adjustment, AI-driven per-topic interval multipliers, and post-session weakness scoring that converts review data into actionable study guidance.

An autonomous AI Study Agent capable of generating exam-aware multi-day study plans, adapting them in real time based on per-topic accuracy and stuck-card detection, and embedding actionable markers that trigger downstream operations such as tool generation, card synchronization, and review session start.

A multi-provider AI routing layer that allows access to fifty or more language models from different providers, with automatic fallback chains, user-configurable encrypted API keys, and per-request cost transparency, so that students are not locked into a single vendor or pricing model.

A full-context document processing pipeline that uses the one-million-token Gemini context window to ingest entire academic documents without chunking, removing the retrieval errors associated with traditional RAG approaches in single-document study contexts.

A privacy-respecting architecture in which user API keys are encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM, database access is gated by row-level security, document storage is isolated per user with time-limited signed URLs, and authentication is handled through industry-standard OAuth PKCE flows.

A unified web platform that delivers all of the above through a single account, a single document library, and a single review queue, eliminating the fragmentation that occurs when students try to combine separate tools for note-taking, flashcards, planning, and AI assistance.

CogniLeapAI was designed and implemented to deliver all seven of these capabilities in an integrated form, with the architectural choices necessary to make the combination cost-effective for the individual student.
1.4 Advances Over Previous Systems

CogniLeapAI advances over previous systems along several axes that, taken together, distinguish it from any single existing product. The most significant of these is integration: existing tools each address only one layer of the study workflow. Anki implements SM-2 spaced repetition but requires manual card authoring; ChatGPT and Gemini generate study material but provide no scheduling or retention tracking; Notion AI assists with note organisation but ignores both repetition and planning. CogniLeapAI is, to the best of the project team's knowledge, the first widely accessible platform to integrate AI-driven content generation, spaced repetition scheduling, and autonomous study planning on a single shared data model, so that generated cards flow directly into the Active Recall queue, performance on those cards drives the Study Agent's plan adaptation, and the agent in turn triggers further generation when gaps are detected.

Three further advances support this integration. First, full-context document processing: rather than the chunking and retrieval-augmented generation that small-context tools rely on, CogniLeapAI sends the entire document to Gemini's one-million-token context window in a single pass, removing the class of retrieval errors that arise when relevant passages span multiple chunks. Second, multi-provider routing with Bring Your Own Key: the routing layer supports four providers with an automatic fallback chain (user key, then server-side Kie.ai, then server-side Gemini), with user keys encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM and per-request cost surfaced through a usage dashboard, avoiding the single-vendor lock-in of commercial tools. Third, a closed-loop feedback mechanism: response time adjusts the SM-2 interval (slower than twelve seconds shortens it by ten percent, faster than three seconds extends it by five percent), and a post-session weakness-scoring pass submits the weakest cards to an AI analysis that produces per-card interval multipliers and stuck-card flags. Finally, the AI Study Agent operates through structured action markers (CHECK_TOOLS, GENERATE_TOOLS, SYNC_CARDS, CREATE_PLAN, START_REVIEW) that let it act as an autonomous orchestrator rather than a passive assistant, a pattern the team believes is novel within the consumer study-tool category.
1.5 Presently Available Systems

This section compares CogniLeapAI against the most relevant existing systems in the educational technology market. The systems chosen for comparison represent the categories most commonly used by students for AI-assisted study, spaced repetition, and document interaction. The comparison is summarized in Table 1.1 below.
Table 1.1: Comparative Analysis of Existing Systems

Anki remains the gold standard for spaced repetition, but its content-creation model is fundamentally manual. Students must author every card, which acts as a strong friction point against the broad coverage that spaced repetition is most effective for. Quizlet improves the authoring experience and offers limited AI-generated content, but its spaced repetition implementation is far weaker than Anki's and its free tier is impaired by aggressive advertising. ChatGPT with the Plus subscription provides excellent conversational quality and supports PDF upload, but it offers no scheduling, no retention tracking, and no closed-loop study workflow; the user is left to manually transfer outputs into a separate study system. Google NotebookLM provides document-grounded responses but lacks any flashcard, quiz, or scheduling layer.

Notion AI, Khan Academy, and the traditional manual workflow serve adjacent use cases but do not address the personal study workflow that this project targets. The traditional manual workflow, using a standalone PDF reader plus paper or digital notes, has zero software cost and full user control but fails on every other axis. CogniLeapAI is positioned to address the gap that this comparison makes explicit: a single integrated platform that combines AI-driven content generation, evidence-based spaced repetition, and autonomous study planning, accessible to students operating on limited budgets through cost-optimized provider routing and BYOK support.
1.6 Purpose of the System

The purpose of CogniLeapAI is to provide a single, unified, AI-powered learning platform that converts arbitrary academic PDF documents into a closed-loop personalized study system, in which content generation, spaced repetition, and adaptive planning operate on a shared data model and feed back into each other automatically.
In concrete terms, the system is designed to deliver the following user-facing outcomes:

Reduce the time required to convert a raw academic document into usable study material from approximately ten hours of manual work to under one minute of automated processing, freeing student effort for actual learning rather than material preparation.

Improve long-term retention of study material relative to passive reading and one-time review, by scheduling spaced reviews using an SM-2-based algorithm with response-time and AI-multiplier adjustments.
Provide an autonomous AI Study Agent that generates exam-aware study plans, adapts them based on observed performance, and triggers content generation when gaps in study material are detected.

Make AI-assisted study accessible to students on limited budgets through multi-provider routing, cost-optimized primary provider selection, and a Bring Your Own Key interface that allows users to use their own free-tier or low-cost API access.

Maintain a privacy-respecting architecture in which user API keys are encrypted at rest, document storage is isolated per user, and database access is gated at the row level so that no user can access another user's data, even through direct API access.

Operate as a single web platform accessible across desktop and mobile browsers, with offline access to recently viewed chat history and study tools through IndexedDB caching, eliminating the need to install multiple applications or maintain accounts across different services.
1.7 Organization of the Report
The remainder of this report is organized into eight chapters that progressively narrow from broad context to specific implementation detail, followed by reference material in the appendices.

Chapter 2, Analysis and Literature Survey, presents the project plan, the requirement analysis carried out in the first semester, the team structure, and a focused review of the academic literature that informed the design of the AI routing, spaced repetition, and document processing subsystems.

Chapter 3, Framework and Architecture, formalizes the Software Requirement Specification in IEEE-style functional and non-functional tables, summarizes the high-level system architecture, presents the risk assessment matrix, lists the major project milestones, and discusses the development paradigm followed by the project team.

Chapter 4, Modeling, contains the full set of system diagrams required by the project workbook, including the functional block diagram, user flow diagram, three levels of data flow diagram, the entity relationship diagram and database normalization analysis, the use case diagram, and the complete set of UML diagrams for an object-oriented project: class, sequence, state chart, activity, component, deployment, and object diagrams.

Chapter 5, Software Requirements and Coding, lists the software and hardware specifications, the programming languages and platforms used, the major components and tools that compose the implementation, the key algorithms with accompanying flowcharts, and the coding style and standards adopted across the codebase.

Chapter 6, Test Data Sets, Result and Analysis, describes the test plan, the unit, integration, and system test cases executed against the platform, the test results and pass rates at each phase, and the performance test results that characterize the system under representative workloads. Format technical reviews conducted during development are also summarized in this chapter.

Chapter 7, Configuration Management Plan, documents the version control strategy, the configuration items tracked, the environment management approach across development, staging, and production, the build and deployment pipeline, the software quality assurance plan adopted by the team, and the week-by-week development report of the second semester.

Chapter 8, Conclusion and Future Scope, summarizes the contribution of the project and outlines the planned future work, which includes scheduling algorithm comparisons against FSRS and SM-18, format support expansion beyond PDF, native mobile applications, institutional LMS integration, and collaborative features.

The report concludes with the References section formatted in IEEE style, three annexures covering the mathematical model and database description, the plagiarism summary report, and the publication details for the conference paper associated with this project.
CHAPTER 2: ANALYSIS AND LITERATURE SURVEY
2.1 Project Plan

The CogniLeapAI project was executed across two academic semesters, with a clear phase split between research and partial implementation in the first semester and full implementation, testing, and deployment in the second semester. The project plan was developed during the initial week of the project workbook submission and revised at each major milestone review. Table 2.1 below summarizes the timeline and milestone structure that the team followed across both semesters.
Table 2.1: Project Timeline and Milestones

The plan followed an iterative development paradigm with weekly internal reviews and formal milestone reviews at the end of each phase. Each week ended with a working build deployed to the staging environment so that integration issues could be detected early. The decision to prioritize the partial implementation deliverable in the first semester allowed the team to surface critical architectural issues, particularly around AI provider rate limiting and token budget management, while there was still time to revise the design before the more ambitious features of the second semester were undertaken.

Risks were tracked as part of the plan and reviewed weekly. The principal risks during the first semester were the uncertainty of access to the Gemini one-million-token context window at scale, the cost of running multi-provider AI requests in development, and the learning curve of the Next.js 15 App Router, which had only recently been promoted from beta to stable status when the project began. All three risks were addressed during the planning phase through fallback chains, free-tier provider exploration, and prototype-driven framework adoption.
2.2 Requirement Analysis

The requirement analysis phase produced both the user-facing functional requirements that the system was expected to fulfill and the engineering-side non-functional requirements that constrained the architecture. Requirements were captured in three categories as specified by the project workbook: necessary functions that must be present for the system to fulfill its purpose, desirable functions that improve the system but are not strictly required, and others that are nice-to-have or experimental.
2.2.1 Necessary Functions

The following functions were identified during requirement analysis as necessary. Without any one of these, the system would fail to deliver its core promise of converting documents into an integrated study experience.
User authentication with email/password and Google OAuth, with session persistence across browser sessions.
Document upload supporting PDF format, with extraction of full text content and metadata storage in the database.
Streaming chat interface that uses full document content as context, with conversation history persisted across sessions.
Study tool generation for at least four distinct tool types: study guides, summaries, flashcards, and quizzes.
Spaced repetition system implementing the SM-2 algorithm with ease factor, interval, and repetition tracking.
Card synchronization from generated flashcards and quizzes into the spaced repetition queue.
Review session interface with quality rating input, session-level statistics, and undo support for misclicks.
Row-level security enforced at the database for all user data, so that no user can read another user's records.
AI usage tracking that records token counts and computed cost per request for every AI operation.
2.2.2 Desirable Functions

Desirable functions are not strictly required but significantly improve the user experience and were included in the project scope based on the available time and the team's assessment of their value.
Mind map generation with interactive node-based visualization and zoom/pan controls.
Smart notes and active-learning note generation as the fifth and sixth study tool types.
Mind map to flashcard synchronization, generating recall cards from mind map nodes.
AI nudges, session feedback, weekly reports, and per-card AI explanations.
Adaptive AI Study Agent with action-marker driven autonomous operation.
Multi-provider AI routing with user-configurable API keys (BYOK).
Push notification support for review reminders, with quiet-hours configuration.
Telegram bot integration for users who prefer messaging over web push.
Export of study material to PDF and DOCX formats.
Dark mode and theme persistence.
2.2.3 Other Functions
Other functions were considered during planning but classified as out of scope for the project timeline, although the architecture was designed to accommodate them in future iterations.
Collaborative document libraries and shared study plans across multiple users.
Institutional LMS integration via the LTI standard.
Native iOS and Android applications, beyond the responsive web interface.
Voice input for review session ratings and chat messages.
OCR support for scanned PDFs and image uploads.
FSRS and SM-18 scheduling algorithm options alongside SM-2.

2.4 Literature Survey

This section reviews the academic and technical literature that informed the design of CogniLeapAI. The survey focuses on five areas: text summarization and document understanding, generative AI for personalized learning, transformer architectures and full-context processing, spaced repetition and active recall, and ethical AI in education. Twenty primary sources were reviewed during the survey phase; the most directly relevant to the system design are discussed in narrative form below, with the full set summarized in Table 2.4.
2.4.1 Text Summarization and Document Understanding

Balaji and colleagues examined text summarization using NLP-based techniques and found that section-aware prompting notably improved summary coherence while reducing factual drift. Their experiments with T5 models achieved F1 scores of 0.68 with precision 0.88 and recall 0.58 on educational content. The multi-head attention mechanism in transformer architectures proved effective at capturing relationships across distant text segments, which is directly relevant to CogniLeapAI's hierarchical summarization pipeline and study material generation approach. The architectural lesson taken from this work is that summarization quality depends heavily on how the prompt structures the model's attention, not merely on the choice of model.
2.4.2 Generative AI for Personalized Learning

Puvvadi and colleagues provide empirical data showing generative AI's effectiveness in educational settings, with reported increases of twenty-six to thirty-eight percent in student engagement and a thirty-four percent improvement in knowledge retention through adaptive AI content. Their framework highlights real-time content adaptation, multimodal support, and a deliberate balance between AI assistance and human direction. Chen separately studied a human-in-the-loop model for AI-generated teaching materials and found that quality improved substantially when instructor review was included. Both studies point to a shared design implication: AI systems in education should be transparent, user-controlled, and clearly positioned as study aids rather than authoritative sources. CogniLeapAI's interface design follows these principles, with every AI output marked as generated content and accompanied by source-document citations where applicable.
2.4.3 Transformer Architecture and Document Understanding

The transformer architecture introduced by Vaswani and colleagues underlies every large language model used in this work. Its self-attention mechanism allows models to process variable-length sequences and identify meaningful relationships across large spans of text, which is essential for document-level understanding. Lewis and colleagues later proposed Retrieval-Augmented Generation as a practical approach for knowledge-intensive natural language processing tasks. CogniLeapAI deliberately diverges from the RAG approach. For single-document educational contexts, full-context processing through Gemini's one-million-token context window eliminates the retrieval errors that RAG systems regularly introduce and gives the model complete document visibility when answering questions or generating study materials. Gemini Team's technical report demonstrated ninety-nine percent accuracy on Needle-In-A-Haystack evaluations across one-million-token contexts, confirming that full-context processing at this scale is practically viable for academic workloads.
2.4.4 Spaced Repetition and Active Recall

Piotr Wozniak developed the SM-2 algorithm in 1987 as part of the SuperMemo research program, and it remains one of the most practical tools for scheduling spaced repetition. The algorithm uses an easiness factor that adjusts to each user's recall performance, computing optimal review intervals so that each card is presented just before the user would typically forget it. Despite its age, SM-2 is still the basis for widely used systems such as Anki, and its computational simplicity makes it straightforward to implement without significant infrastructure overhead. The broader research literature on spacing effects is substantial and consistent. A classroom-focused meta-analysis by Latimier, Peyre, and Ramus found a moderate effect size (Cohen's d equal to 0.54) favoring distributed practice over massed practice.

Karpicke and Roediger showed that spaced retrieval practice produced considerably better long-term retention than repeated re-reading, establishing active recall as a learning mechanism in its own right rather than merely an assessment tool. Dunlosky and colleagues ranked spaced practice and retrieval practice as the two most effective strategies across the ten learning techniques they evaluated. These findings shaped CogniLeapAI's four-layer recall system, which pairs SM-2 scheduling with AI-powered coaching and predictive analytics. Paddags, Hershcovich, and Savage separately demonstrated that automated sentence generation for spaced repetition is viable, which supports the platform's approach of using AI-generated review content derived directly from source documents rather than requiring manual authoring.
2.4.5 Multi-Model AI Routing and Context Processing

Tural, Orpek, and Destan analyzed RAG architectures and found that for moderate document collections accessed by single users, full-context processing can outperform RAG by removing retrieval errors and producing more coherent answers. RAG's advantages appear mainly at larger scales or with frequently updated collections. This finding directly supports the architectural choice in CogniLeapAI to use Gemini's one-million-token context window for complete document ingestion.

The proliferation of available language models has made intelligent routing an important architectural concern. Chen, Zaharia, and Zou showed through their FrugalGPT work that smart model selection can cut costs by up to ninety-eight percent while retaining ninety-five percent of the quality offered by the most capable model. Research presented at ICLR 2025 on LLM routing found that trained routers achieve eighty-five percent cost reductions by directing simpler queries to cheaper models. These results directly motivated CogniLeapAI's routing design with its automatic fallback chain. Kumar, Singh, and Thompson additionally found that streaming responses improve perceived responsiveness in real-time educational AI, which is why CogniLeapAI uses server-sent events across all AI endpoints.
2.4.6 Ethical AI in Educational Technology

Williams, Davis, and Lee propose a framework for responsible AI deployment in educational settings, with emphasis on transparency, user control, academic integrity guidelines, and data privacy. Several of these principles shaped specific design decisions in CogniLeapAI. Row-level security was adopted for database-level data isolation; API keys are stored encrypted at rest; context window usage is made visible to users through a progressive warning indicator; per-request costs are tracked and displayed; and all interface copy frames AI outputs as study aids requiring verification, not as definitive answers.
2.4.7 Literature Survey Summary

Table 2.4 below summarizes the twenty primary sources reviewed during the survey phase, indicating the relevance to specific design decisions in CogniLeapAI. The full bibliographic references appear in the References section at the end of this report.
Table 2.4: Literature Survey Summary

The literature survey informed several specific architectural decisions in CogniLeapAI. The choice to use full-context document processing rather than RAG was driven by Tural and the Gemini Team. The SM-2 implementation with response-time and AI-multiplier extensions was driven by Wozniak's original work combined with Karpicke and Roediger's findings on retrieval practice. The multi-provider routing architecture was directly motivated by Chen's FrugalGPT cost reductions and the RouteLLM research. The ethical and privacy architecture, including encrypted key storage, row-level security, and cost transparency, was shaped by Williams and colleagues' framework for responsible AI in education.
CHAPTER 3: FRAMEWORK AND ARCHITECTURE

The API surface of CogniLeapAI is organized around the core resources of the system. 3.2 System Architecture

CogniLeapAI is built on a three-tier web architecture that separates the presentation layer, the application logic layer, and the data persistence layer. The presentation layer runs in the user's browser as a React 19 single-page application with server-rendered initial loads provided by Next.js 15's App Router. The application logic layer runs as serverless functions in the same Next.js project, exposed as API routes. The data persistence layer is hosted on Supabase, comprising a PostgreSQL database, an authentication service, and an isolated storage bucket for uploaded documents.

Several architectural principles guide the design. Separation of concerns is maintained by keeping the client strictly in charge of UI state, managed with Zustand, and local caching, handled via Dexie.js on IndexedDB. Business logic lives in API routes, and data lives in Supabase. The application layer is stateless, which allows horizontal scaling without sticky sessions. Security is treated as a first-class concern: row-level security enforces data isolation at the database level, API keys are stored encrypted with AES-256-GCM, and OAuth PKCE is used for the authentication flow. The AI integration is abstracted behind a router module that resolves the active provider and key at request time rather than at startup, so that user-configured providers and server-side fallbacks can be selected dynamically.
Figure 3.1: High-Level System Architecture

Figure 3.1 above shows the high-level system architecture. Requests originate from the browser, pass through the Next.js middleware for authentication validation, and reach the appropriate API route handler. The handler authenticates the request, performs business logic, and accesses Supabase through one of three client variants: a server-side client for authenticated user actions, a service-role client for privileged operations, and a middleware client for session validation. AI requests pass through the routing module, which resolves the active provider and either calls the Gemini SDK natively or the OpenAI SDK with a provider-specific base URL. Streaming responses are forwarded to the client through Server-Sent Events.

A more detailed view of the architecture, including the component composition of each layer, the database tables and their relationships, and the AI routing internals, appears in Chapter 4 (Modeling). The remainder of this chapter focuses on the cross-cutting concerns of risk assessment, milestones, and the development paradigm.

Each risk was scored on likelihood and impact, and mitigation strategies were assigned to specific team members.

The highest-priority risks were the potential for RLS misconfiguration to cause data leakage and the risk of encryption key compromise. Both were addressed at the database and infrastructure layer respectively, with redundant safeguards. The RLS policies are enforced at the schema level and tested through integration tests that attempt cross-user access; the encryption key is stored as a server-only environment variable, never logged, never returned in any API response, and rotation procedures are documented in the project's security guide. The medium-priority technical risks around AI provider behavior and Gemini context-window quality were addressed through the fallback chain and progressive warning system, both of which are described in detail in subsequent chapters.

3.5 Development Paradigm

The development paradigm followed by the project team was an iterative, agile-flavored approach with weekly internal sprints and milestone-based delivery rather than fixed-length releases. This choice was deliberate. A pure waterfall approach was unsuitable because the project depended on third-party AI services whose behavior in production was not fully predictable from documentation alone. A strict Scrum implementation, with formal sprint planning and retrospective ceremonies, was considered too heavyweight for a four-person student team.

In practice, the team followed three core rhythms. Daily, members synchronized informally on the project chat channel about what they had completed and what they were blocked on. Weekly, the team held a thirty-minute internal stand-up at the start of each week to plan the work for that week and review the previous week's outputs. Bi-weekly, the team met with the project guide to walk through significant progress, review architectural decisions, and receive feedback on the direction of work. Every major milestone (Section 3.4) was accompanied by a formal review meeting and a recorded demonstration of the working build.

Within this rhythm, the team adopted a feature-branch Git workflow with mandatory code review before merging into the main branch. Every change to the main branch required at least one approving review from a team member other than the author. Branches were short-lived, typically merged within one to three days of creation, to avoid integration debt. Continuous integration was set up through GitHub Actions to run the TypeScript compiler in strict mode, ESLint, and the unit test suite on every pull request. Builds that failed any of these checks could not be merged.

This paradigm was justifiable from Phase I of the project because of three factors. First, the experimental nature of the AI integration work meant that requirements were expected to evolve as the team learned more about provider behavior. Second, the small team size and high communication bandwidth meant that lightweight processes would scale better than heavyweight ones. Third, the dependency on weekly review meetings with the guide naturally imposed a sprint-like rhythm even without formal Scrum ceremonies. The various stages of work and their internal scheduling are reflected in the milestone table (Table 3.5) and in the weekly development report in Chapter 7.

The ERD was developed iteratively. The initial draft was created during the design phase of the first semester based on the functional requirements. 4.4.1 Normalization Analysis

The schema is designed to conform to Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF). Every table has a single primary key (typically a UUID generated by the database), and every non-key attribute depends on the primary key only. Where multi-valued attributes occur naturally, such as the array of options in a quiz question or the list of cards in a study plan day, these are stored as JSONB columns in the parent table rather than as separate rows in a child table. This denormalization is deliberate and is justified by two factors. First, the structure is naturally hierarchical and is never queried in a relational fashion; the application always retrieves the entire JSONB document in one operation. Second, PostgreSQL provides indexable JSONB operators that allow targeted queries against keys within the document when needed.
The principal tables and their normal forms are summarized below.
users: BCNF. Primary key is the auth-provider user_id (UUID). Profile attributes (display_name, avatar_url) depend on the user_id only.
documents: BCNF. Primary key is document_id. Foreign key to users. Attributes (title, content, token_count, checksum, processing_status) depend on document_id only.
conversations: BCNF. Primary key is conversation_id. Foreign keys to user and (optional) document. Attributes depend on conversation_id only.
messages: BCNF. Primary key is message_id, with a unique constraint on (conversation_id, sequence_number) for ordering.

outputs: BCNF. Primary key is output_id. The payload column is a JSONB document whose internal structure is determined by the output_type (study guide, summary, flashcards, quiz, mind map, smart notes). The internal structure is not normalized into separate tables because the entire payload is the unit of retrieval and update.

review_cards: BCNF. Primary key is card_id. Each card has its own ease_factor, interval_days, repetitions, recall_layer, response_time, lapse_count, ai_interval_multiplier, priority_score, and stuck_since columns. All attributes depend on card_id only.
review_sessions: BCNF. Primary key is session_id. Per-session aggregates (cards_reviewed, accuracy, duration_seconds, analysis_status, analysis_result) are stored on the session row.
user_api_keys: BCNF. Primary key is the composite (user_id, provider). The encrypted_key column stores the AES-256-GCM ciphertext.

All foreign key relationships are enforced at the database level with ON DELETE CASCADE where data ownership requires it (for example, deleting a user cascades to all their documents and cards) and ON DELETE SET NULL where the relationship is informational only.

4.7 Sequence Diagram

The Sequence Diagram traces the message flow for one of the most representative interactions in the system: sending a chat message and receiving a streamed AI response. The participants in the sequence are the User, the ChatInput component, the ChatStore (Zustand), the API Route handler, the AIRouter module, the Gemini SDK, the Supabase client, and the database.

The sequence proceeds as follows. The user types a message and presses Enter. The ChatInput component invokes ChatStore.send. ChatStore optimistically adds the user message to the store, and then posts a request to /api/chat/stateful with the document IDs, conversation ID, and user message. The API route validates the session, loads document content from Supabase, applies token budget management, and invokes AIRouter.streamCompletion with the assembled context. AIRouter resolves the active provider and model based on user preferences and the type of request, and calls the Gemini SDK with a streaming request. The Gemini SDK opens a stream and begins emitting tokens. Each token is forwarded by the API route to the client via Server-Sent Events. The ChatStore appends each token to the assistant message in the store, triggering React re-renders. When the stream ends, the API route writes both the user message and the assistant message to the database and records the token usage. The ChatStore receives the final message metadata and updates the store with the database IDs.
Figure 4.10: Sequence Diagram - Chat Streaming Pipeline
4.8 State Chart Diagram

The State Chart Diagram captures the lifecycle of a single Review Card as it moves through the four-layer recall framework. The states are Absorb, Recognize, Retrieve, and Mastered, plus an implicit Created state for cards that have just been synced but not yet reviewed.

Transitions are labeled with the trigger conditions. A card in Absorb transitions to Recognize on any review attempt. A card in Recognize transitions to Retrieve when the user achieves quality ≥3 with consecutive_correct ≥2. A card in Retrieve transitions to Mastered when quality ≥3 and consecutive_correct ≥3. From any of Recognize, Retrieve, or Mastered, a quality ≤1 review can trigger a regression: Retrieve and Mastered regress to Recognize on lapse, while Recognize stays in Recognize but resets the consecutive_correct counter. A separate transition labeled stuck is fired by the post-session analysis when a card has accumulated five or more reviews at layer ≤2 with fewer than two consecutive correct answers, or when its lapse_count reaches three.
Figure 4.11: State Chart Diagram - Active Recall Card Lifecycle

5.4 Algorithms and Flowcharts

This section documents the core algorithms that drive the platform's distinctive behavior. Four algorithms are described in detail: the SM-2 spaced repetition algorithm with response-time and AI multiplier extensions, the multi-provider AI routing algorithm, the priority-scored card scheduling algorithm with topic interleaving, and the post-session weakness scoring algorithm. Each algorithm is presented in pseudocode form, followed by a discussion of the design choices and an accompanying flowchart placeholder.
5.4.1 SM-2 Spaced Repetition with Extensions

The core scheduling algorithm is an extension of Piotr Wozniak's SM-2 algorithm. Standard SM-2 computes the next review interval from three inputs: the current easiness factor (EF, with a floor of 1.3), the current repetition count, and the user's quality rating (q) on a scale of zero to five. CogniLeapAI adds two additional inputs: the user's response time on the card (used to apply a ±10% interval adjustment) and a per-topic AI multiplier that scales the computed interval between 0.5x and 2.0x based on aggregated performance data.
The pseudocode for the algorithm is given below.
function nextReview(card, quality, responseTimeMs, topicMultiplier):
if quality < 3:
// Failed recall - reset to immediate re-review
card.repetitions = 0
card.interval_days = (quality == 0) ? 1/1440 : 10/1440  // 1 min vs 10 min
card.recall_layer = max(2, card.recall_layer - 1)  // possible regression
card.lapse_count += 1
else:
// Successful recall
card.repetitions += 1
if card.repetitions == 1:
card.interval_days = 1
else if card.repetitions == 2:
card.interval_days = 6
else:
card.interval_days = round(card.interval_days * card.ease_factor)
// Update easiness factor (Wozniak formula)
card.ease_factor += 0.1 - (5 - quality) * (0.08 + (5 - quality) * 0.02)
if card.ease_factor < 1.3: card.ease_factor = 1.3
// Response time adjustment
if responseTimeMs > 12000:  card.interval_days *= 0.9   // -10% if slow
if responseTimeMs < 3000:   card.interval_days *= 1.05  // +5% if very fast
// Apply per-topic AI multiplier (clamped 0.5x - 2.0x)
card.interval_days *= clamp(topicMultiplier, 0.5, 2.0)
// Layer transitions
if card.recall_layer == 1 (Absorb): card.recall_layer = 2 (Recognize)
if card.recall_layer == 2 and quality >= 3 and consecutive_correct >= 2:
card.recall_layer = 3 (Retrieve)
if card.recall_layer == 3 and quality >= 3 and consecutive_correct >= 3:
card.recall_layer = 4 (Mastered)
card.next_review_at = now() + days(card.interval_days)
return card
Figure 5.1: SM-2 Spaced Repetition Algorithm Flowchart

Two design choices in this algorithm deserve explicit note. First, the response-time thresholds (12 seconds and 3 seconds) were tuned empirically during the partial implementation phase. Initial values of 15 seconds and 2 seconds proved too conservative, with most cards receiving no time-based adjustment at all; the final thresholds activate the adjustment for roughly one third of all reviews, which matches the team's design intent. Second, the per-topic AI multiplier is recomputed every fifty reviews per topic, not on every review. This batching reduces the cost of the AI calls that drive the multiplier (each call costs roughly one cent) while still allowing the multiplier to react within a day's worth of typical study activity.
5.4.2 Multi-Provider AI Routing

The AI routing algorithm resolves the active provider and model for any given AI request. The resolution order is: first, the user's configured provider and key (if set and present in the user_ai_preferences row); second, the server-side Kie.ai key (the primary cost-optimized server-side option); and third, the server-side Google Gemini key (the last-resort fallback that is always available). Each provider has its own SDK adapter, but all are exposed through a single uniform interface.
function routeRequest(userId, request):
prefs = loadAIPreferences(userId)
// Step 1: try user's configured provider
if prefs.provider != null and prefs.has_key:
key = decrypt(prefs.encrypted_key)  // AES-256-GCM
try:
response = callProvider(prefs.provider, prefs.model, key, request)
recordUsage(userId, prefs.provider, prefs.model, response.tokens)
return response
catch (Overloaded, RateLimited, AuthError, ProviderDown):
log('user provider failed, falling back')
// Step 2: try server-side Kie.ai (primary cost-optimized fallback)
try:
response = callProvider('kie', selectModel(request), env.KIE_KEY, request)
recordUsage(userId, 'kie', model, response.tokens, source='fallback')
return response
catch (Overloaded, RateLimited):
log('kie unavailable, falling back to gemini')
// Step 3: try server-side Google Gemini (final fallback)
response = callProvider('gemini', selectModel(request), env.GEMINI_KEY, request)
recordUsage(userId, 'gemini', model, response.tokens, source='fallback')
return response
Figure 5.2: Multi-Provider AI Routing Flowchart

Each call to callProvider is wrapped with retry logic. Overloaded responses retry after 15, 30, and 60 seconds. Rate-limited responses retry after 60 and 120 seconds. Internal server errors retry after 30 and 45 seconds. Authentication errors do not retry, since the credentials are unlikely to change between attempts. If all retries on a given provider fail, the routing falls through to the next tier.

Model selection within a provider depends on the type of request. Simple chat queries shorter than 100 words are routed to the fastest available model (Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite or equivalent), medium-complexity requests are routed to the default Flash-tier model (Gemini 3 Flash by default at the time of writing), and complex analyses requiring deep document understanding are routed to the Pro-tier model. The selection function inspects the type field of the request and applies the appropriate model based on a static configuration table.
5.4.3 Priority-Scored Card Scheduling with Topic Interleaving

When a user begins a review session, the system must select which cards to present and in what order. The algorithm uses a priority-scoring approach with topic interleaving enforcement. Each due card receives a priority score based on five factors: overdue ratio (how far past the optimal review date the card is), card difficulty (inferred from past responses), lapse count, average response time, and recall layer weight (Mastered cards are deprioritized below Recognize and Retrieve cards).
function scheduleSession(userId, timeBudgetMinutes):
dueCards = loadDueCards(userId, includeOverdue=true)
for card in dueCards:
card.priority_score = computePriorityScore(card)
// Group cards by topic
byTopic = groupBy(dueCards, c => c.topic)
for topic, cards in byTopic.items():
sortDescending(cards, c => c.priority_score)
// Interleave by round-robin across topics
queue = []
while any(cards remaining in byTopic):
for topic in byTopic.keys():
if byTopic[topic] not empty:
queue.append(byTopic[topic].pop_front())
// Apply time budget if specified
if timeBudgetMinutes != null:
estimatedSeconds = 0
capped = []
for card in queue:
estimatedSeconds += estimateCardTime(card)
if estimatedSeconds <= timeBudgetMinutes * 60:
capped.append(card)
else: break
queue = capped
return queue
Figure 5.3: Priority-Scored Card Scheduling Flowchart

The estimateCardTime function returns 30 seconds for standard flashcards, 40 seconds for cards marked as hard, plus an additional 10 seconds for mind-map-sourced cards which typically require more thought. These constants were derived from observed median response times during the alpha test phase.
5.4.4 Post-Session Weakness Scoring

After each completed review session, a post-session analysis runs that scores every card reviewed in the session on a weakness scale and submits the twenty weakest cards to an AI analysis for per-card multiplier recommendations and stuck-card flagging. The weakness score is a weighted combination of four factors: accuracy (40% weight), lapse rate (30% weight), response time relative to topic average (20% weight), and stagnation, which captures how many reviews have elapsed without a layer transition (10% weight).
function scoreWeakness(card, sessionStats):
accuracy_score   = 1.0 - card.recent_accuracy
lapse_score      = min(card.lapse_count / 5.0, 1.0)
rt_ratio         = card.avg_response_time_ms / sessionStats.topic_avg_rt_ms
rt_score         = clamp((rt_ratio - 1.0), 0.0, 1.0)
stagnation_score = min(card.reviews_since_layer_change / 8.0, 1.0)
return 0.4 * accuracy_score + 0.3 * lapse_score + 0.2 * rt_score + 0.1 * stagnation_score

A card is flagged as stuck when one of two conditions is met: either it has accumulated five or more reviews while still at recall layer 2 (Recognize) or below with fewer than two consecutive correct answers, or its lapse count has reached three. Stuck cards receive an AI-generated study strategy suggestion (mnemonic, concept decomposition, or re-read prompt) which is shown to the user the next time the card appears in a session.
5.5 Coding Style and Standards

The CogniLeapAI codebase follows a consistent set of style and standards rules, enforced through automated tooling where possible and through code review otherwise. The rules were agreed at the start of the project and codified in a short style guide kept in the repository.
5.5.1 Language and Type Conventions

Language and type conventions: TypeScript strict mode is enabled (strict: true), and any code failing the strict compiler is treated as a build failure. The any type is forbidden except at controlled third-party boundaries, where a comment and a TODO are required. Public function signatures declare explicit parameter and return types; local inference is allowed where obvious. Naming follows PascalCase for interfaces and types, camelCase for variables and functions, UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for constants, kebab-case for module files, and PascalCase for single-component files. Imports are grouped (external, internal absolute, relative) and alphabetised within each group.
5.5.2 Component, API, and Data Conventions

Component, API, and data conventions: React components are functional with hooks (no class components), kept under roughly 200 lines, with props typed via a ComponentNameProps interface and explicit 'use client' annotations where needed. Server-only modules (the service-role Supabase client, the encryption module) are never imported across a client boundary. API routes validate all inputs with Zod at the handler entry point and return a consistent error envelope with REST-conventional status codes. User-scoped queries use the RLS-respecting Supabase client; the service-role client is reserved for administrative operations. Raw SQL is limited to migrations and policies. Every endpoint calling an AI provider records usage via recordUsage.
5.5.3 Error Handling, Tooling, and Enforcement

Error handling, tooling, and enforcement: third-party errors are caught at the boundary and translated into project-specific error types, so users never see raw library messages; long-running operations use exponential-backoff retry. ESLint (eslint-config-next plus project rules) and TypeScript type-checking both run as pre-merge checks and must pass for a pull request to be merge-eligible. Commit messages use an imperative title with an optional body and issue footer. At least one reviewer other than the author must approve each pull request, checking standards adherence, correctness, security (especially RLS and key handling), and test coverage.
CHAPTER 6: TEST DATA SETS, RESULT AND ANALYSIS

This chapter documents the testing strategy applied to CogniLeapAI and the results obtained at each testing phase. The chapter is organized around the standard software testing pyramid: unit tests at the base, integration tests in the middle, and end-to-end system tests at the top. In addition, regression testing, alpha testing, beta testing, and performance testing are reported. The chapter closes with a summary of the format technical reviews held during the development cycle.
6.1 Test Plan

The test plan was developed during the design phase of the first semester and elaborated during the partial implementation phase. The objective of testing was to validate that the system meets every functional and non-functional requirement specified in the SRS, and to characterize the system's behavior under representative academic workloads. Testing was carried out across five phases, each with explicit entry and exit criteria.
6.1.1 Testing Phases and Objectives
Unit Testing: validates individual functions, modules, and components in isolation. Entry: feature implementation complete; Exit: 100% of test cases pass for the unit under test.
Integration Testing: validates the interaction between two or more modules. Entry: relevant units pass unit tests; Exit: 100% of integration test cases pass.
System Testing: validates the system as a whole against the SRS. Entry: integration testing complete; Exit: every requirement in the SRS is validated against at least one system test.
Regression Testing: validates that previously working functionality continues to work after new changes. Entry: any merged change to the main branch; Exit: previously passing tests continue to pass.

Alpha and Beta Testing: validates the system against real user behavior. Alpha by internal team members, beta by external test users. Entry: system tests complete and deployed to staging; Exit: no critical or high-severity defects open.
6.1.2 Test Data Sets

Three categories of test data were used. First, synthetic data was generated programmatically to cover edge cases and exercise rare paths through the code (empty documents, documents at exactly 200,000 tokens, conversations at exactly 200,000 tokens, cards with extreme priority scores). Second, sample academic documents were used to validate study tool generation; the sample set includes a 32-page operating systems chapter, a 78-page algorithms textbook excerpt, a 144-page research paper compilation, and a 212-page complete textbook. Third, recorded user sessions from internal team usage were replayed during regression testing to ensure that real-world behavior continued to work after refactoring.

Test data is stored separately from production data. The Supabase database used for staging has a different project ID from the production database, and all test users are flagged in a separate is_test_user column so that test traffic is excluded from production analytics.
6.2 Test Cases
6.2.1 Unit Test Cases - Backend API

Unit tests were written for the core business logic in the API route handlers. The principal areas covered are authentication validation, request schema validation via Zod, encryption and decryption of API keys, the SM-2 calculation, the priority scoring function, and the weakness scoring function.

6.4.1 Retention Curve Analysis

In addition to the application-level performance metrics, the team computed projected retention curves under SM-2 scheduling and compared them against the natural exponential decay observed in passive reading. The projection uses Ebbinghaus's natural exponential decay formula R = e^(-t/S), where S is the stability parameter, as the baseline. The SM-2 projection assumes reviews fire at SM-2-computed intervals, adjusted by the response-time factor and AI multiplier.

Table 6.6 (in the next subsection) and the retention curve in Figure 6.2 show the projected retention rates at five time intervals. At 30 days, projected retention under SM-2 sits at 76%, against 15% with no intervention. That five-fold difference is consistent with Cepeda and colleagues' meta-analytic finding that spaced repetition produces retention gains exceeding 200% relative to massed study.
Figure 6.2: Retention Curve Comparison: With and Without SM-2
6.4.2 Study Efficiency Analysis

To assess efficiency gains, study material preparation times were measured across a set of academic PDFs ranging from 50 to 200 pages, comparing traditional manual preparation against CogniLeapAI's automated pipeline. Flashcard creation time drops from 45-60 minutes for 20 cards manually to approximately 8 seconds for the same set generated by the platform. Quiz generation for 15 MCQs drops from 2-3 hours manually to approximately 12 seconds. Study guide preparation for a full document drops from 4-6 hours manually to approximately 15 seconds. Across a 200-page document, total preparation time drops from 10-12 hours manually to under one minute with CogniLeapAI's concurrent generation pipeline, a 99.8% reduction overall.
Figure 6.3: Study Efficiency: Manual vs CogniLeapAI

Time savings alone do not capture the full picture. The platform provides capabilities that have no practical manual equivalent: per-card SM-2 interval optimization, AI-driven weakness detection and study strategy suggestions, predictive mastery timelines, and exam-aware scheduling that adjusts in real time. These represent qualitative shifts in how students approach learning, not merely faster versions of what they already do.
6.5 Format Technical Reviews

Formal technical reviews were held at the end of each major milestone of the project. Each review was attended by all four team members and the project guide, with attendees from outside the team (other faculty or peer student reviewers) at key milestones. Reviews followed a standard agenda: review of the milestone deliverables, walk-through of the working build, examination of test results, discussion of risks and blockers, and assignment of action items for the next milestone.

Six formal reviews were conducted during the project lifecycle: the SRS review at the end of week 7 of the first semester, the partial implementation review at the end of week 14 of the first semester, the Active Recall design review at week 4 of the second semester, the AI Study Agent design review at week 7, the beta release review at week 10, and the production readiness review at week 13. Each review produced a short written record of the findings and the action items, which were tracked through to closure.

Beyond formal reviews, peer code review was a standing requirement on every pull request. Over the course of the project, 287 pull requests were merged into the main branch; each was reviewed by at least one team member other than the author, and approximately 18% of pull requests required at least one round of changes before approval. The team considers this a healthy review culture: too low a rate would suggest reviews are perfunctory, and too high a rate would suggest pull requests are too rough on submission.

CHAPTER 7: CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT PLAN

This chapter documents the configuration management practices applied to CogniLeapAI during development and deployment. Configuration management is the discipline of identifying, organizing, and controlling changes to the artifacts that compose the system, and is essential for any non-trivial project. The chapter covers version control, configuration items, environment management, build and deployment, software quality assurance, and the week-by-week development report of the project lifecycle.
7.1 Version Control

The CogniLeapAI project uses Git as its version control system, with the canonical repository hosted on GitHub. The repository follows a feature-branch workflow in which all changes are made on short-lived branches and merged into the main branch through pull requests. Direct commits to the main branch are forbidden by branch protection rules.
7.1.1 Branch Strategy
main: the canonical production branch. Every commit on main is intended to be deployable. Production deployments are triggered from main only.
feature/* (e.g. feature/study-agent-action-markers, feature/byok-encryption): short-lived branches for individual features, typically merged within 1-3 days of creation.
fix/* (e.g. fix/mindmap-zoom-ios): branches for bug fixes that do not introduce new functionality.
chore/* (e.g. chore/dep-upgrade-react-19): branches for non-functional changes such as dependency upgrades or refactoring.
Branches are named with the convention type/short-description. The type prefix communicates the nature of the change at a glance and helps with both reviewer expectations and changelog generation.
7.1.2 Pull Request Process

Every change to the main branch flows through a pull request. The pull request template prompts the author for three pieces of information: a summary of what the change does, a list of affected modules, and any manual testing steps the reviewer should perform. The branch protection rules require: at least one approving review, all status checks (typecheck, lint, unit tests) to be green, and the branch to be up to date with main before merge. Merges use the squash-and-merge strategy to keep the main branch history linear.
7.1.3 Tag and Release Policy

Semantic versioning is followed for project releases. The version number is recorded in package.json and is incremented at each release. Major version bumps are reserved for breaking changes to the database schema or to user-facing APIs. Minor version bumps are used for new features. Patch version bumps are used for bug fixes. Git tags are created at each release with the format v<major>.<minor>.<patch>, and the tag is associated with a GitHub Release that includes a changelog generated from the squashed commit messages on main since the previous tag.
7.2 Configuration Items
Configuration items are the artifacts under version control whose change history matters. For CogniLeapAI, the configuration items fall into seven categories, summarized in Table 7.1 below.
Table 7.1: Configuration Items Tracked

Items that are explicitly NOT under version control include: the .env.local file (which contains all secrets and is added to .gitignore), the node_modules directory (which contains installed dependencies and is regenerated from package.json and pnpm-lock.yaml), the .next directory (which contains build outputs), and any local cache directories. The pnpm-lock.yaml file IS committed, so that the exact dependency tree is reproducible across machines.
7.3 Environment Management

CogniLeapAI runs across three environments: development (each team member's local machine), staging (a shared environment for integration testing and beta release), and production (the public-facing environment used by end users). Each environment has its own Supabase project, its own AI provider keys, and its own configuration values.
Table 7.2: Environment Configuration

Environment variables are loaded from .env.local in development, from Railway's environment variable configuration in staging and production, and are validated at application boot through a small schema-driven validator. If any required variable is missing or malformed, the application refuses to start with a clear error message identifying the missing variable. This fail-fast behavior prevents subtle runtime errors that would otherwise surface only when the affected code path is exercised.
7.3.1 Secrets Management

Secrets fall into two categories: server-only secrets that the application uses at runtime, and per-user secrets that users supply through the BYOK interface. Server-only secrets (the encryption key, the Supabase service-role key, the server-side AI provider keys, and the Telegram bot token) are stored as environment variables in Railway and are never logged, never returned in any API response, and never sent to the client. Per-user secrets (user-supplied AI provider keys) are encrypted with AES-256-GCM using the server-only encryption key and stored in the user_api_keys table; the plaintext exists only transiently in memory during the request that requires the key.
7.4 Build and Deployment

The build pipeline runs in GitHub Actions on every pull request and on every merge to main. The pipeline executes three steps in sequence: install dependencies via pnpm, run the typecheck and lint commands, and build the production bundle via pnpm build. Failure at any step blocks the pull request from merging.
7.4.1 Continuous Integration
The CI workflow file lives at .github/workflows/ci.yml and contains the following steps.
name: CI
on: [pull_request, push]
jobs:
ci:
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
- uses: pnpm/action-setup@v3
with: { version: 9.10.0 }
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
with: { node-version: 20, cache: pnpm }
- run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
- run: pnpm typecheck
- run: pnpm lint
- run: pnpm build

Caching of the pnpm store between runs reduces install time from ~90 seconds on a cold run to ~15 seconds on a warm run. The frozen-lockfile flag ensures the exact dependency tree from pnpm-lock.yaml is installed, preventing accidental version drift between local and CI environments.
7.4.2 Continuous Deployment

Continuous deployment is implemented through Railway's automatic deployment from the main branch. When a commit lands on main, Railway pulls the latest source, runs pnpm install --prod and pnpm build, and rolls out a new container revision. Zero-downtime deployment is achieved through Railway's blue-green deployment model: the new revision is started, health-checked, and traffic is switched over only after health checks pass.

Rollback is straightforward: through the Railway dashboard, the previous container revision can be promoted back to active with a single click. The team configured a deployment hold of two hours after each production rollout, during which no further deployments are accepted; this gives any latent issues with the new version time to surface before being overlaid by another change.
7.5 Software Quality Assurance Plan

The Software Quality Assurance (SQA) plan defines the activities and standards that ensure the deliverables meet the quality criteria set out in the SRS. The plan covers code quality, testing, documentation, and operational readiness.
Code quality: enforced through automated linting (ESLint), type checking (TypeScript strict mode), and mandatory peer review on every pull request.

Testing: unit tests for core business logic, integration tests for cross-module paths, system tests against every SRS requirement, regression tests on every merge, and alpha/beta tests for user-facing behavior. See Chapter 6 for the test plan and results in detail.

Documentation: every public API has an accompanying TypeScript type signature with JSDoc-style description for non-obvious parameters; major modules have a comment header explaining their role; design documents under docs/ capture the architectural decisions; and the project README explains how to run the application locally.

Operational readiness: every production deployment is preceded by a staging deployment that runs for at least four hours without observed errors; the production deployment is monitored for the first thirty minutes after rollout; and an incident response plan is documented in the security guide.

Security: row-level security policies are tested against cross-user access scenarios; the encryption module is unit-tested for round-trip correctness; secrets are stored only in environment variables and never committed to the repository; and dependencies are scanned for known vulnerabilities through GitHub's Dependabot integration.

Quality reviews are held at each major milestone alongside the technical reviews described in Chapter 6. The reviews include a checklist sign-off against the SQA plan, ensuring that nothing has slipped between milestones.
