CHAPTER 8: CONCLUSION AND FUTURE SCOPE
8.1 Conclusion

This report has presented CogniLeapAI, an adaptive personalized learning platform that combines multi-model AI routing, evidence-based spaced repetition, and autonomous study planning to convert static educational PDFs into a closed-loop learning system. The project was conceived to address a clearly identified gap in the educational technology landscape: the absence of an integrated, cost-accessible, privacy-respecting platform that delivers AI-driven content generation alongside rigorous learning science for the individual student. Across two academic semesters, the team designed, implemented, tested, and deployed a working production system that addresses each of the unmet needs identified in Chapter 1.

The principal technical contributions of the project are five. First, a multi-model AI routing layer with automatic three-tier fallback (user-configured provider, server-side Kie.ai, server-side Gemini) maintains a measured 99.5% AI request success rate while keeping costs under control through cost-optimized primary provider selection. Second, full-context document processing using Gemini's one-million-token context window removes the retrieval errors associated with traditional Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipelines in single-document study contexts. Third, the four-layer SM-2 Active Recall framework with response-time-aware interval adjustment, per-topic AI multipliers, and post-session weakness scoring operationalizes the spacing effect literature into a production system that requires no manual card authoring. Fourth, the AI Study Agent with action-marker driven autonomous operation orchestrates content generation, card synchronization, and plan adaptation through a single conversational interface. Fifth, the system's privacy architecture (AES-256-GCM encryption of user API keys, row-level security on all user-scoped tables, OAuth PKCE authentication, isolated storage buckets) demonstrates that strong privacy guarantees and AI-powered functionality are not in tension.

Functionally, the platform delivers six study material types (summaries, study guides, active-learning notes, flashcards, quizzes, mind maps) generated concurrently from any uploaded document, with all generated material flowing automatically into a unified spaced repetition queue. Performance benchmarks across representative academic workloads consistently met or exceeded the targets specified in the SRS. Study material preparation time drops from a typical 10-12 hours of manual work for a 200-page document to under one minute through CogniLeapAI's concurrent generation pipeline, a 99.8% reduction. Projected retention curves under SM-2 scheduling, supported by the spaced repetition literature, sit at 76% retention after 30 days against 15% with no intervention.

What separates CogniLeapAI from comparable tools, and what the team considers the project's most distinctive contribution, is the Study Agent. To the best of the team's knowledge, no existing widely accessible system connects AI content generation, spaced repetition scheduling, and autonomous study planning into one integrated pipeline. The agent discovers gaps in study materials, generates what is missing, synchronizes new cards into the recall system, and rebuilds study plans around live performance data. The cost of running the system is kept manageable through Kie.ai as the primary provider and BYOK support for users who prefer direct API access. Usage analytics make every cost decision visible to the user.

Taken together, the features delivered demonstrate that advanced AI-driven educational tools need not be expensive, technically complex, or locked behind institutional access. A four-person student team, working within the time constraints of two academic semesters and the budget constraints of a self-funded project, was able to build, test, deploy, and document a production-ready system that compares favorably against commercial products with substantial engineering teams behind them.
8.2 Future Scope

Several improvements are planned for subsequent development phases, targeting both current limitations of the system and new capability areas that emerged during user testing. The future scope is organized into four areas: document processing expansion, learning science enhancements, platform expansion, and ecosystem integration.
8.2.1 Document Processing Expansion

In-application source viewer: a side-by-side document viewer that allows users to browse the reference material directly alongside AI conversations, with PDF page navigation, image pan and zoom, and DOCX preview, enabling immediate cross-reference between AI outputs and source content.
DOCX upload via mammoth.js: format support will be extended to Microsoft Word documents, which represent a substantial fraction of academic material that is not natively PDF.

Image upload through Gemini's multimodal capabilities: photographs of handwritten notes, whiteboards, and printed material that students may have outside of PDF form will be supported as first-class document sources.

OCR for scanned PDFs through Tesseract.js integration: scanned documents and image-based PDFs currently return poor extraction results from text-only parsers. Tesseract.js integration will address this and dramatically broaden the range of usable source documents.
8.2.2 Learning Science Enhancements

FSRS and SM-18 algorithm comparison: a comparative study of SM-2 against the Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler and SuperMemo's SM-18 is planned to determine whether either produces measurably better outcomes for the platform's user base. Either algorithm could be offered as a user-selectable alternative to SM-2.

Cross-topic interleaving and retrieval practice scheduling: more sophisticated review session design that mixes topics deliberately based on cognitive load theory rather than purely on per-topic priority scores.

Personalized interval prediction: the retention history and decay rate data already stored in learning_analytics will be used for more personalized interval predictions, moving beyond the fixed SM-2 formula toward per-user, per-topic learned models.
Mastery decay modeling: long-term retention beyond the Mastered layer will be modeled explicitly, with refresher prompts surfaced before predicted decay rather than reactively after lapses.
8.2.3 Platform Expansion

Progressive Web Application (PWA) support: offline access to cached study materials and review cards will be enabled through Service Worker caching, removing the internet dependency for revision sessions on commutes and in low-connectivity environments.

Native mobile applications: iOS and Android applications, built with React Native, are under consideration for a later phase. The team will evaluate whether the responsive web interface plus PWA support is sufficient before committing to native development.

Collaborative features: shared document pools and group study plans will support peer learning. A team-study feature would allow study groups to share progress, compare retention curves, and review each other's weak topics.
Voice input: voice-based card ratings and chat input through the Web Speech API will support hands-free review during commutes.
8.2.4 Ecosystem Integration

Institutional LMS integration: deployment in formal academic environments through the LTI 1.3 standard, allowing instructors to assign documents and review windows through their existing Moodle, Canvas, or Blackboard installations.
Browser extension: a lightweight browser extension that lets users save web pages directly into their CogniLeapAI document library, alongside the existing PDF upload flow.

Public API for developers: opening the platform's content generation and spaced repetition primitives to third-party developers through a documented REST API and SDK, enabling a broader ecosystem of complementary tools.
Calendar integration: bidirectional sync with Google Calendar and Microsoft Outlook so that scheduled study sessions appear alongside other academic commitments.

Each of these planned enhancements has been scoped against the existing architecture, and the database schema, AI routing layer, and review pipeline have all been designed to accommodate them without major rework. The team intends to continue developing CogniLeapAI beyond the project submission, both as a public service and as a basis for future research into AI-assisted education.

ANNEXURE A: MATHEMATICAL MODEL AND DATABASE DESCRIPTION
A.1 Mathematical Model
This annexure formalizes the principal mathematical components of CogniLeapAI's spaced repetition and predictive analytics subsystems.
A.1.1 SM-2 Easiness Factor Update
Given the current easiness factor EF and the user's quality rating q on a zero-to-five scale, the updated easiness factor EF' is computed as:
EF' = max(EF', 1.3)   // floor enforced

The constant 0.1 represents the maximum increase in EF for a perfect recall (q=5), while the term (5 - q) * (0.08 + (5 - q) * 0.02) penalizes lower recall qualities with a quadratic penalty. The minimum floor of 1.3 prevents the easiness factor from collapsing toward zero, which would cause review intervals to shrink without bound.
A.1.2 Interval Calculation
For the n-th successful repetition (q >= 3), the interval I_n in days is:
I_n = round(I_{n-1} * EF)        for n >= 3

For unsuccessful recall (q < 3), the interval is reset to a short value (one minute for q=0, ten minutes for q=1 or q=2) and the repetition counter is reset to zero. Layer regression may also apply, as described in Chapter 5.
A.1.3 Response-Time Adjustment
Let t be the user's response time in milliseconds and I the computed interval. The adjusted interval I' is:
I' = I * 0.90   if t > 12000 ms   (slow response, shorten by 10%)
I' = I * 1.05   if t < 3000 ms    (fast response, extend by 5%)
I' = I          otherwise
A.1.4 AI Multiplier Application

The per-topic AI multiplier m is recomputed every fifty reviews per topic by an AI analysis run at temperature 0.3 (deterministic). The multiplier is clamped to the range [0.5, 2.0] and applied to the response-time-adjusted interval:
I_final = I' * clamp(m, 0.5, 2.0)
A.1.5 Weakness Score
For each card reviewed in a session, the weakness score W is a weighted sum of four normalized factors:
W = 0.40 * (1 - accuracy_recent)
+ 0.30 * min(lapse_count / 5.0, 1.0)
+ 0.10 * min(reviews_since_layer_change / 8.0, 1.0)

where rt_ratio is the card's average response time divided by the topic's average response time, accuracy_recent is the proportion of correct responses over the card's last five reviews, and reviews_since_layer_change is the count of reviews since the card last changed recall layer. All four factors range over [0, 1], so W also ranges over [0, 1].
A.1.6 Retention Forecast
The platform projects retention using the Ebbinghaus exponential decay model with the stability parameter S derived from the card's SM-2 state:
R(t) = exp(-t / S)
S = max(interval_days * EF, 1.0)

Each successful review at the optimal point resets the predicted retention to approximately 90-95% and extends the stability parameter, which is how review intervals grow over time. The day-by-day forecast over a configurable window (default 30 days) is computed by evaluating R(t) at each day boundary for each card and aggregating across cards to produce average expected retention and cards due on each day.
A.2 Database Description

CogniLeapAI uses Supabase PostgreSQL with over twenty-five tables grouped into five domains: authentication and users, documents and content, chat, study tools and active recall, and analytics. Table A.1 summarizes the principal tables. All tables are protected by Row Level Security policies enforcing auth.uid() = user_id (or equivalent through a join) on every operation.
Table A.1: Principal Database Tables
A.2.1 Migration Strategy

Schema changes are managed through versioned migration files in supabase/migrations/. Each migration file is timestamped (YYYYMMDD format prefix) and named for the change it introduces. Migrations are forward-only; rollback is achieved by writing a new migration that undoes the previous change. The full migration sequence at final report submission consists of twenty-one migrations spanning from the initial documents and conversations schema (January 2025) through the adaptive learning system migration (March 2026). The migrations are applied in order through Supabase's migration tooling, which records the applied migration set in a system table to prevent re-application.

Index design prioritizes the access patterns of the most-used queries: messages are indexed by (conversation_id, sequence_number) for ordered retrieval; review cards are indexed by (user_id, next_review_at) for due-card scheduling; outputs are indexed by (document_id, section_id, type) for tool retrieval; and usage records are indexed by (user_id, created_at) for the usage analytics dashboard. Partial indexes are used where they reduce index size meaningfully, for example on documents.checksum where the column is nullable.

C.3 Paper Abstracts
C.3.1 ICETES-2026 Paper Abstract

The ICETES paper presents CogniLeapAI as an AI-powered platform that transforms static PDF documents into a closed-loop adaptive learning system. The system combines multi-model AI routing across Google Gemini, OpenRouter, LaoZhang, and Kie.ai, six study-tool generators, a four-layer SM-2 spaced repetition framework, predictive retention forecasting, and an Adaptive AI Study Agent for exam-aware planning and real-time schedule adaptation. The implementation uses Next.js 15, React 19, Supabase, encrypted API-key storage, row-level security, and per-request usage tracking. Field testing reports significant time savings in study-material preparation and improved engagement through active recall.
C.3.2 IJEDR Paper Abstract

The IJEDR paper presents CogniLeapAI as a web-based learning platform that routes AI tasks to suitable Gemini model tiers, performs semantic document search within browser privacy constraints, and automatically generates summaries, flashcards, study guides, and annotated notes. It reports end-to-end chat responses under 500 ms, semantic search completion under 200 ms, stable operation across a thousand concurrent sessions, and a zero-recurring-cost architecture using free-tier cloud services and local computation.

Server-side functions that run before a request reaches a page or API route handler. Used in CogniLeapAI for authentication validation and session refresh.
Proof Key for Code Exchange, an extension to OAuth 2.0 that mitigates authorization code interception attacks. Used by Supabase Auth for Google sign-in.

A PostgreSQL feature that allows access control policies to be enforced at the row level, so that a query automatically filters out rows the user is not authorized to read, regardless of the application logic.
A standard for streaming text data from a server to a browser over a single HTTP connection. Used by CogniLeapAI for streaming AI responses from the API route to the client.

The second algorithm in the SuperMemo family, designed by Piotr Wozniak in 1987. It schedules spaced repetition based on an easiness factor and the user's recall quality. The basis of Anki and of CogniLeapAI's review scheduling.
A learning technique in which review intervals are progressively increased between successful recalls, exploiting the spacing effect to maximize long-term retention per unit of study time.
A chat interface in which the AI response appears progressively, token by token, as it is generated, rather than appearing all at once after the full response is computed.

A card flagged by the post-session analysis as unable to progress, either because it has accumulated five or more reviews at layer 2 or below with fewer than two consecutive correct answers, or because its lapse count has reached three.
A unit of text used by a language model, typically corresponding to roughly four characters or three-quarters of a word in English. The context window of a language model is measured in tokens.
A bundler optimization in which unused exports from a module are removed from the production bundle, reducing the bundle size delivered to the browser.
A lightweight client-side state management library for React, used in CogniLeapAI for application-wide state such as chat history, study tools, and review sessions.
